name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَٱلْمُرْسَلَـٰتِ عُرْفًا (١)
```

```english
By the [angelic] emissaries sent successively, (1)
```

```arabic
فَٱلْعَـٰصِفَـٰتِ عَصْفًا (٢)
```

```english
by those who sweep along like gale, (2)
```

```arabic
وَٱلنَّـٰشِرَٰتِ نَشْرًا (٣)
```

```english
by those who publish [the Divine messages] far and wide, (3)
```

```arabic
فَٱلْفَـٰرِقَـٰتِ فَرْقًا (٤)
```

```english
by those who separate [the truth from falsehood] distinctly, (4)
```

```arabic
فَٱلْمُلْقِيَـٰتِ ذِكْرًا (٥)
```

```english
by those who inspire [God’s] remembrance, (5)
```

```arabic
عُذْرًا أَوْ نُذْرًا (٦)
```

```english
as exemption or warning: (6)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَا تُوعَدُونَ لَوَٰقِعٌ (٧)
```

```english
what you are promised will surely befall. (7)
```

```arabic
فَإِذَا ٱلنُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ (٨)
```

```english
When the stars are blotted out (8)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا ٱلسَّمَآءُ فُرِجَتْ (٩)
```

```english
and the sky is cleft, (9)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا ٱلْجِبَالُ نُسِفَتْ (١٠)
```

```english
when the mountains are scattered [like dust] (10)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا ٱلرُّسُلُ أُقِّتَتْ (١١)
```

```english
and time is set for the apostles [to bear witness] (11)
```

```arabic
لِأَىِّ يَوْمٍ أُجِّلَتْ (١٢)
```

```english
—for what day has [all] that been set [to occur]? (12)
```

```arabic
لِيَوْمِ ٱلْفَصْلِ (١٣)
```

```english
For the Day of Judgement! (13)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا يَوْمُ ٱلْفَصْلِ (١٤)
```

```english
And what will show you what is the Day of Judgement!? (14)
```

```arabic
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (١٥)
```

```english
Woe to the deniers on that day! (15)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah mentions the Day of Decision (يوم الفصل), which is the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة), confirming the report of its occurrence and coupling it with a severe threat (الوعيد) for those who deny it, as well as a warning (الإنذار) and glad tidings (التبشير) for others. The aspect of the threat predominates in the Surah, as His saying: **وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ** (Woe to the deniers on that day!) is repeated ten times.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالْمُرْسَلاتِ عُرْفاً** **By the [angels] sent forth successively (1)** and the verses following it until the completion of six verses, are an oath (إقسام) from Him, the Exalted, by matters expressed as "those sent forth" (المرسلات), then "the tempestuous" (العاصفات), then "the spreaders" (الناشرات), then "the separators" (الفارقات), then "the deliverers" (الملقيات) of a reminder (ذكراً), as an excuse (عذراً) or a warning (نذراً). The first two—namely, "those sent forth successively" (المرسلات عرفاً) and "the tempestuous ones" (العاصفات عصفاً)—if left to themselves without considering the context, would appear to refer to successive winds blowing violently. However, the last one—namely, "the deliverers of a reminder, as an excuse or a warning"—is almost explicit in referring to the angels (الملائكة) descending upon the messengers (الرسل), carrying the revelation (الوحي) of the message and delivering it to them to complete the proof (إتمام الحجة) or as a warning. The remaining attributes do not resist being interpreted in a manner consistent with this meaning.

To interpret all five attributes as referring to winds—as is apparent in "those sent forth" and "the tempestuous"—requires great affectation (تكلف) in justifying the remaining three attributes, especially the final one.

Similarly, interpreting "those sent forth" and "the tempestuous" as winds while interpreting the remaining three, or the last two, or only the final one as the angels of revelation (ملائكة الوحي) is problematic, as there is no apparent affinity between winds and the angels of revelation such that they would be paired in the oaths and organized into a single sequence. The various justifications offered for this are far-fetched meanings that do not occur to the mind at the opening of the discourse without prior notification.

Therefore, the correct approach is to disregard these opinions—which are numerous and nearly impossible to categorize—and interpret the mentioned entities as the angels of revelation, similar to their counterparts at the beginning of Surah al-Saffat: **وَالصَّافَّاتِ صَفًّا فَالزَّاجِراتِ زَجْراً فَالتَّالِياتِ ذِكْراً** **By those [angels] set in ranks, (1) by those who drive away [the clouds] (2) by those who recite the Reminder (3)** [Qarai: 37:1-3]. In the same vein is His saying, the Exalted: **عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ فَإِنَّهُ يَسْلُكُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ رَصَداً لِيَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا رِسالاتِ رَبِّهِمْ** **Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, (26) except to an apostle He approves of. Then He dispatches a sentinel before him and behind him, (27) so that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord...** [Qarai: 72:26-28].

So His saying: **وَالْمُرْسَلاتِ عُرْفاً** is an oath by them. The word *‘urf* (العرف) refers to the hair growing on the neck of a horse, and it is used as a metaphor for things when they follow one another; it is said: "They came like the mane of a horse" (كعرف الفرس). It is also used metaphorically to say: "The sandgrouse came *‘urfan*" (عرفاً), meaning successively, and "They came to him as one *‘urf*" (عرفاً واحداً), meaning in succession. *Al-‘Urf* (العرف) also means that which is "recognized" (المعروف) of commands and prohibitions. Thus, *‘urfan* (عرفاً) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) according to the first meaning, and an object of purpose (مفعول له) according to the second. Sending (الإرسال) is the opposite of withholding (الإمساك). The feminine form of "those sent forth" (المرسلات) is used in consideration of the groups (الجماعات) or in consideration of the Spirit (الروح) with which the angels descend. He, the Exalted, said: **يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ** **He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command onto whomever He wishes of His servants...** [Qarai: 16:2], and He said: **يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ** **...He casts the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants...** [Qarai: 40:15].

The meaning is: I swear by the groups sent forth from the angels of revelation.

It has been said that "those sent forth successively" refers to the successive winds that are sent, but the weakness of this view has already been noted. Equally weak is the view that it refers to the Prophets (الأنبياء) عليه‌السلام, as it does not harmonize with what follows it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَالْعاصِفاتِ عَصْفاً** **by the tempestuous ones (2)** is coordinated (عطف) with "those sent forth." The word *‘asf* (العصف) refers to speed of travel, a metaphor derived from the "tempestuousness" (عصف) of winds—meaning the speed of their blowing—referring to the speed of their travel toward that for which they were sent. The meaning is: I swear by the angels who are sent successively and who hasten in their travel like tempestuous winds.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالنَّاشِراتِ نَشْراً** **by the spreaders (3)** is another oath. To "spread" (نشر) a scroll, a book, a garment, or the like is to unfold it. What is meant by "spreading" here is the unfolding of the scrolls of revelation (صحف الوحي), as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **كَلَّا إِنَّها تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَنْ شاءَ ذَكَرَهُ فِي صُحُفٍ مُكَرَّمَةٍ مَرْفُوعَةٍ مُطَهَّرَةٍ بِأَيْدِي سَفَرَةٍ كِرامٍ بَرَرَةٍ** **No indeed! It is a reminder (11) —so let anyone who wishes remember it— (12) [recorded] in honored scrolls, (13) exalted and purified, (14) by the hands of envoys, (15) noble and pious.** [Qarai: 80:11-16]. The meaning is: And I swear by the angels who spread the scrolls upon which the revelation is written for the Prophet to receive it.

It has been said that it refers to the winds that Allah, the Exalted, spreads before His mercy; or the winds that spread the clouds; or the angels who spread the scrolls of deeds (صحائف الأعمال); or the angels who spread their wings upon descending; and other things have been said as well.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَالْفارِقاتِ فَرْقاً** **by the separators (4)** refers to the separation (الفرق) between truth (الحق) and falsehood (الباطل), and between the lawful (الحلال) and the prohibited (الحرام). This "separation" is an attribute branching out from the aforementioned "spreading."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَالْمُلْقِياتِ ذِكْراً عُذْراً أَوْ نُذْراً** **by the deliverers of the Reminder, (5) as an excuse or warning: (6)** What is meant by the "Reminder" (الذكر) is the Quran which they recite to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, or revelation in general descending upon the Prophets to whom it is recited.

The three attributes—namely, spreading (النشر), separating (الفرق), and delivering the Reminder (إلقاء الذكر)—are sequential. The separation between truth and falsehood, and between the lawful and the prohibited, is realized through the spreading of the scrolls and the delivery of the Reminder. Through spreading, the separation begins to be realized, and through recitation, its realization is completed. Thus, spreading results in a degree of the existence of separation, and upon that follows the completion of its existence through delivery.

His saying: **عُذْراً أَوْ نُذْراً** (as an excuse or warning) are both objects of purpose (مفعول له), and "or" (أو) is for categorization (التنويع). It is said they are two verbal nouns (مصدران) meaning "excusing" (الإعذار) and "warning" (الإنذار). Excusing is to provide that by which one becomes excused (معذوراً). The meaning is that they deliver the Reminder so that it may be an excuse for His believing servants through the Reminder, and a frightening (تخويف) for others.

It has also been said: so that it may be an excuse by which Allah excuses Himself to His servants regarding punishment—that it was only done according to wisdom—which amounts to the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة). Thus, the sum of the meaning according to this is that they deliver the Reminder to be a completion of the proof against the deniers and a warning to others, which is a fine meaning.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّما تُوعَدُونَ لَواقِعٌ** **Indeed what you are promised will surely come to pass. (7)** is the response to the oath (جواب القسم). The word "what" (ما) is a relative pronoun (موصولة), and the address is to all of humanity. What is meant by "what you are promised" is the Day of Resurrection with its punishment and reward. The word "occurring" (الواقع) is more emphatic than "being" (الكائن) because of the nuance of stability (الاستقرار) it carries. The meaning is that which Allah has promised you of resurrection, punishment, and reward will inevitably be realized.

## ( A Discourse on His Oaths in the Quran )

Among the subtle artistry of eloquence (صنعة البيان) in these six verses is that, while they contain oaths to emphasize the report in the response, they also contain the proof (الحجة) for the content of the response—namely, the occurrence of the promised recompense (الجزاء). For the Divine governance (التدبير الربوبي) to which the oath refers—namely, the sending of those sent forth tempestuously, their spreading of the scrolls, their separating, and their delivering the Reminder to the Prophet—is a governance that is not complete except with the existence of Divine obligation (التكليف الإلهي). And obligation is not complete except with the necessity of a day prepared for recompense, in which the disobedient and the obedient among the subjects of obligation are recompensed.

Thus, the very governance by which He, the Exalted, swore to emphasize the occurrence of the promised recompense is itself a proof of its occurrence. It is as if it were said: I swear by this proof that its implication is a reality.

If you contemplate the instances where the oath appears in His speech, the Exalted, and examine them deeply, you will find that the object of the oath (المقسم به) is a proof indicating the truth of the response (الجواب). For example, His saying regarding sustenance (الرزق): **فَوَ رَبِّ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ** **By the Lord of the sky and the earth, it is indeed the truth...** [Qarai: 51:23], for the Lordship (ربوبية) of the heaven and the earth is the principle (المبدأ) for the sustenance of those provided for. And His saying: **لَعَمْرُكَ إِنَّهمْ لَفِي سَكْرَتِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ** **By your life, they were bewildered in their drunkenness.** [Qarai: 15:72], for the pure life of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, protected by the infallibility (عصمة) of Allah, is indicative of their drunkenness and bewilderment. And His saying: **وَالشَّمْسِ وَضُحاها** **By the sun and its morning brightness, (1)** —until He said— **وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها وَقَدْ خابَ مَنْ دَسَّاها** **and by the soul and Him who fashioned it, (7) and inspired it with [discernment between] its virtues and vices: (8) one who purifies it is certainly felicitous, (9) and one who betrays it is certainly a failure.** [Qarai: 91:1, 7-10]. For this precise system (النظام المتقن) that culminates in the inspired soul which distinguishes its vices and virtues is the evidence for the success of the one who purifies it and the failure of the one who corrupts it.

Following this pattern are the rest of the oaths mentioned in His speech, the Exalted, even if some of them are not without a degree of obscurity that requires deep reflection, such as His saying: **وَالتِّينِ وَالزَّيْتُونِ وَطُورِ سِينِينَ** **By the Fig and the Olive, (1) by Mount Sinai, (2)** [Qarai: 95:1-2]. It is upon you to contemplate them.

* * *

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِذَا النُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ** **So when the stars are put out, (8)** —until His saying— **أُقِّتَتْ** **[when] the apostles are given their time, (11)** is an exposition of the promised Day whose occurrence was reported in His saying: **إِنَّما تُوعَدُونَ لَواقِعٌ** (Indeed what you are promised will surely come to pass). The response to "when" (إذا) is omitted, indicated by His saying: **لِأَيِّ يَوْمٍ أُجِّلَتْ** **For what day was [all this] deferred? (12)** —until His saying— **لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ** **Woe to the deniers on that day! (15)**.

He, the Glorified, has defined the promised Day by mentioning occurring events that are inseparable from the extinction of the human world and the cessation of the worldly system (النظام الدنيوي), such as the extinguishing of the stars, the splitting of the earth, the crumbling of the mountains, and the transformation of the system into another system that differs from it. This has been repeated in many Quranic Surahs, especially the short ones like Surah al-Naba, al-Nazi‘at, al-Takwir, al-Infitar, al-Inshiqaq, al-Fajr, al-Zilzal, al-Qari‘ah, and others. These mentioned matters have been counted in the narrations (الأخبار) as among the signs of the Hour (أشراط الساعة).

It is known by necessity (بالضرورة) from the expositions of the Book and the Sunnah that the system of life in all its affairs in the Hereafter (الآخرة) is different from its system in this world (الدنيا). The Abode of the Hereafter is an eternal abode (دار أبدية) containing pure felicity (السعادة) for its inhabitants—they have therein whatever they wish—or pure wretchedness (الشقاء), and they have therein only what they loathe. The worldly abode is an abode of transience and cessation (دار فناء وزوال) where only external, apparent causes and factors govern, where death is mixed with life, loss with finding, wretchedness with felicity, toil with rest, and sorrow with joy. The Hereafter is an abode of recompense (دار جزاء) and no action (عمل), while the world is an abode of action and no recompense. In short, the realm (النشأة) is different from the realm.

Thus, His defining the realm of resurrection and recompense by its signs—which involve the folding of the carpet of the world through the destruction of the structure of its earth, the uprooting of its mountains, the splitting of its sky, and the extinguishing of its stars, among other things—is like defining one realm by the collapse of the system governing another realm. He, the Exalted, said: **وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ النَّشْأَةَ الْأُولى فَلَوْ لا تَذَكَّرُونَ** **Certainly you have known the first genesis, then why do you not take admonition?** [Qarai: 56:62].

So His saying: **فَإِذَا النُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ** means their trace of light and other things is erased. *Al-Tams* (الطمس) is the removal of a trace by erasing. He, the Exalted, said: **وَإِذَا النُّجُومُ انْكَدَرَتْ** **when the stars are dimmed,** [Qarai: 81:2].

And His saying: **وَإِذَا السَّماءُ فُرِجَتْ** **when the sky is split open, (9)** means it is rent asunder. *Al-Farj* (الفرج) and *al-Furjah* (الفرجة) is the gap between two things. He, the Exalted, said: **إِذَا السَّماءُ انْشَقَّتْ** **When the sky is split open,** [Qarai: 84:1].

And His saying: **وَإِذَا الْجِبالُ نُسِفَتْ** **when the mountains are scattered [as dust], (10)** means they are uprooted and removed, from the saying: "The wind *nasafat* (نسفت) the thing," meaning it uprooted and removed it. He, the Exalted, said: **وَيَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْجِبالِ فَقُلْ يَنْسِفُها رَبِّي نَسْفاً** **They ask you concerning the mountains. Say, ‘My Lord will hollow them out [into dust].** [Qarai: 20:105].

And His saying: **وَإِذَا الرُّسُلُ أُقِّتَتْ** **[when] the apostles are given their time, (11)** means the time is appointed for them to be present to bear witness over the nations, or they have reached the time they were waiting for to deliver their testimony over the nations, from *ta’qit* (التأقيت) meaning *tawqit* (التوقيت) — *the appointment of a specific time*. He, the Exalted, said: **فَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الْمُرْسَلِينَ** **We will surely question those to whom [the apostles] were sent, and We will surely question the apostles.** [Qarai: 7:6], and He said: **يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ اللهُ الرُّسُلَ فَيَقُولُ ما ذا أُجِبْتُمْ** **The day Allah will gather the apostles and say, ‘What was the response you received?’...** [Qarai: 5:109].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لِأَيِّ يَوْمٍ أُجِّلَتْ لِيَوْمِ الْفَصْلِ وَما أَدْراكَ ما يَوْمُ الْفَصْلِ وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ** **For what day was [all this] deferred? (12) For the Day of Decision. (13) And what will show you what the Day of Decision is? (14) Woe to the deniers on that day! (15)** *Al-Ajal* (الأجل) is the period fixed for a thing, and *al-ta’jil* (التأجيل) is the setting of a term for a thing. It is used for its necessary consequence, which is "deferment" (التأخير), as in the saying: "a deferred debt" (دين مؤجل), meaning it has a term, as opposed to "immediate" (الحال). This meaning is most suitable for the verse. The pronoun in "deferred" (أُجِّلَتْ) refers to the matters mentioned previously: the extinguishing of the stars, the splitting of the sky, the uprooting of the mountains, and the timing of the messengers. The meaning is: For what day were these matters deferred?

It is possible that "deferred" (أُجِّلَتْ) means the fixing of a term for a thing, and that the implied pronoun in it refers to the messengers, or to what the speech suggests of matters related to the messengers—such as what they reported of the states of the Hereafter, its terrors, the punishment of the disbelievers, and the blessing of the believers therein. None of this is without some obscurity.

The verse and the one following it—namely, His saying: **لِأَيِّ يَوْمٍ أُجِّلَتْ لِيَوْمِ الْفَصْلِ**—are cast in the form of a question and its answer for the purpose of glorification (التعظيم), making it formidable (التهويل), and expressing wonder (التعجيب). The original meaning is: These matters were deferred for the Day of Decision.

This type of interrogative sentence is in the sense of an implied statement (تقدير القول). The meaning is that due to the greatness of this day, its terror, and its being a wonder, it is asked: For what day were these great, terrifying, and wondrous matters deferred? Then it is answered: For the Day of Decision.

His saying: **لِيَوْمِ الْفَصْلِ** is the Day of Recompense in which the judgment is decided (فصل القضاء). He, the Exalted, said: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ** **...Indeed Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection...** [Qarai: 22:17].

His saying: **وَما أَدْراكَ ما يَوْمُ الْفَصْلِ** is a glorification of the Day and an aggrandizement of its affair.

His saying: **وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ** — *Al-Wayl* (الويل) is destruction (الهلاك). What is meant by "the deniers" (المكذبين) are those who deny the Day of Decision in which there is what they are promised, for the verses are directed toward explaining its occurrence, and He has sworn that it is a reality.

In the verse is an invocation against the deniers, and it has made the mention of the response to "when" (إذا) in His saying: **فَإِذَا النُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ** etc., unnecessary. The estimation (التقدير) is: "When such and such happens, the punishment you were promised for denial will occur," or the estimation is: "When such and such happens, it will be the Day of Decision and the deniers of it will perish."

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Khisal*, from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: Abu Bakr said: "Grayness has hastened to you, O Messenger of Allah." He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: **شيبتني هود والواقعة والمرسلات وعم يتساءلون** — "Surahs Hud, al-Waqi‘ah, al-Mursalat, and ‘Amma Yatasa’alun have made me gray."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Bukhari, Muslim, al-Nasa’i, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Mas‘ud, who said: "While we were with the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in a cave at Mina—when Surah *wa al-Mursalat ‘Urfa* was revealed to him—he was reciting it and I was receiving it from his mouth—and his mouth was still moist with it—when a snake leaped at him. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: **اقتلوها** — 'Kill it.' So we rushed toward it, but it escaped. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: **وقيت شركم كما وقيتم شرها** — 'It has been protected from your evil just as you have been protected from its evil.'"

**I say:** It has also been narrated through two other chains.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَالْمُرْسَلاتِ عُرْفاً**, he said: "Verses that follow one another."

In *al-Majma‘*: Regarding the verse, it was said: "They are the angels sent with the 'recognized' (المعروف)—of the command of Allah and His prohibition." This is in the narration of al-Harawi from Ibn Mas‘ud, and from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali from the companions of ‘Ali, from him عليه‌السلام.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِذَا النُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ**, he said: "Their light goes away and they fall."

In the same source, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: **فَإِذَا النُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ**: "Their extinguishing is the departure of their light." **وَإِذَا السَّماءُ فُرِجَتْ**: He said: "It opens and splits." **وَإِذَا الرُّسُلُ أُقِّتَتْ**: He said: "They were sent at different times."

In *al-Majma‘*, Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام said: "**أُقِّتَتْ** means they were sent at different times."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لِأَيِّ يَوْمٍ أُجِّلَتْ**, he said: "Deferred."
